does: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of fine-tuning paradigms for large vision-language models using deep reinforcement learning and direct preference optimization. It examines how DRL enables models to optimize through reward signals rather than supervised data alone, while DPO directly aligns model policies with human preferences without requiring explicit reward models. The work categorizes key approaches for using these techniques to improve LVLMs across dimensions including human value alignment, task-specific performance, and adaptive multimodal interaction. It systematically explores sources of preference data, reward signal design, and implementation considerations for applying both DRL and DPO frameworks to the unique challenges of aligning vision-language systems.
connects: This work builds on the extensive literature of aligning large language models through reinforcement learning and preference optimization, extending established techniques like RLHF and DPO to the multimodal setting. It responds to challenges identified in prior work on supervised fine-tuning limitations and the importance of incorporating human feedback signals beyond standard pretraining objectives. The paper connects to research on reward modeling, preference learning, and reinforcement learning from human feedback that has matured in pure language settings. It also engages with concurrent work on multimodal pretraining and the specific challenges of understanding both visual and textual content. The overview synthesizes insights from preference elicitation, inverse reinforcement learning, and recent developments in offline RL that inform how to effectively learn from human judgments.
enables: This framework enables more sophisticated alignment of multimodal systems with nuanced human preferences across diverse visual and textual domains. It opens pathways for developing LVLMs that can adapt to context-specific tasks and user values without extensive task-specific retraining. The systematic categorization of approaches facilitates future work on combining DRL and DPO strengths to address failure modes in either method alone. It points toward research on scalable preference annotation for multimodal content, designing interpretable reward functions for vision-language tasks, and understanding how alignment in one modality transfers across modalities. The overview also enables investigation of whether multimodal alignment presents fundamentally different challenges than unimodal alignment, and how emergent capabilities from visual understanding interact with preference-based fine-tuning.
